title: Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements
subtitle: Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.
caption:
Number of parties in multilateral environmental agreements

Total number of global parties signed on to multilateral agreements designed to address trans-boundary environmental issues.

This line chart tracks the cumulative number of global parties signed on to twelve specific multilateral environmental agreements from 1971 to 2015. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 200 parties, while the horizontal axis marks years in five-year increments. The Vienna Convention (Ozone), represented by a dark blue line, begins earliest around 1971 and rises steadily to reach approximately 195 parties by 2015, sitting near the top of the chart alongside the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC) and the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD), which both approach or exceed 190 parties. Several agreements, including the Kyoto Protocol and the World Heritage Convention, show steep increases starting in the 1990s and plateau near 180 to 190 parties by 2010. In contrast, the Convention on the Conservation of Migratory Species of Wild Animals, shown in teal at the bottom, remains below 130 parties throughout the period. The Stockholm Convention on Persistent Organic Pollutants and the Cartagena Protocol on Biosafety begin after 2000 and rise sharply to around 170 and 160 parties respectively by 2015. Most lines cluster between 150 and 190 parties by the end of the period, indicating broad participation across many agreements. The Ramsar Convention (Wetlands) and the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) follow similar trajectories, reaching approximately 160 to 170 parties. The visual pattern shows that older agreements generally have higher party counts, while newer ones start later but often catch up within two decades.
